quests.title: The Celestial Cartographer
quests.description: Enoch, guided by angels, is tasked with mapping the celestial spheres and recording the movements of the stars and planets. This requires him to journey through the heavens, observing and documenting the divine order to preserve this knowledge for humanity.
quests.outcome: Enoch successfully charts the heavens, leaving behind scrolls of astronomical and astrological wisdom that influence future generations.

quests.title: The Trial of Azazel
quests.description: Enoch is appointed as a judge in the celestial court to witness the trial of the fallen angel Azazel, who corrupted humanity with forbidden knowledge. He must grapple with complex moral issues of free will, divine justice and the consequences of transgression.
quests.outcome: Enoch, after witnessing Azazel's sentencing, gains a deeper understanding of the divine plan and the balance between mercy and judgment.

quests.title: The Ascent Through the Seven Heavens
quests.description: Enoch is chosen to ascend through the seven heavens, each a realm of increasing divine splendor and danger. He learns the secrets of creation, witnesses the angels at their duties, and is purified in preparation for his transformation.
quests.outcome: Enoch successfully traverses the seven heavens, gaining profound spiritual insight and demonstrating unwavering faith, ultimately leading to his transformation.

quests.title: The Rescue of the Watchers' Children
quests.description: Enoch, moved by the plight of the children born from the union of Watchers and mortal women (the Nephilim), pleads before God for their redemption. He acts as an intercessor between the divine and these hybrid beings.
quests.outcome: While unable to fully avert the fate of the Nephilim, Enoch secures a measure of divine mercy and delays their ultimate destruction, showcasing his compassion and moral courage.

displayName: Enoch as Idris in Islamic Tradition

feats.title: Walking with God
feats.description: Enoch lived a life of unparalleled righteousness, constantly striving to understand and follow God's will. He maintained a close relationship with the divine through prayer, meditation, and righteous actions.
feats.significance: This exemplified the ideal of human piety and demonstrated the possibility of a profound connection between humanity and the divine.

feats.title: The Transformation into Metatron
feats.description: Enoch, due to his extraordinary virtue, was taken by God and transformed into the angel Metatron, the highest of the angels and the scribe of God. This transformation involved immense spiritual purification and elevation.
feats.significance: This highlights the potential for human spiritual growth and elevation to a divine status, representing the ultimate reward for righteousness.

feats.title: Authoring the Book of Enoch
feats.description: Enoch, through divine inspiration, authored the Book of Enoch, a collection of prophecies, visions, and teachings about the celestial realms, the fate of humanity, and the coming judgment. He became a source of divine knowledge.
feats.significance: This provided a valuable source of spiritual guidance and understanding for future generations, preserving ancient wisdom and revealing profound truths about the universe.

feats.title: Witnessing the Divine Chariot
feats.description: During his ascent, Enoch was granted the privilege of witnessing the Merkabah, the divine chariot of God. This awe-inspiring vision bestowed upon him great wisdom and understanding of the divine power.
feats.significance: This experience elevated his spiritual understanding, allowing him to become a vessel of God's knowledge and a conduit for divine wisdom to humanity.

companions.name: Angels of Guidance
companions.description: Enoch, known for his wisdom and righteousness, was often accompanied by angels who guided him through celestial realms and revealed divine secrets. They served as teachers and protectors during his ascensions.

companions.name: Metatron
companions.description: In some traditions, particularly Kabbalistic ones, Enoch is transformed into the angel Metatron. As Metatron, he stands before God's throne, acting as a protector and intermediary.

companions.name: Angelic Scribes
companions.description: Enoch is described as a scribe, recording divine knowledge. Angelic scribes may have assisted him in transcribing and preserving the secrets of the heavens and the prophecies he received.

companions.name: Noah
companions.description: Enoch is considered to be Noah's great-grandfather. He is seen as a moral example and spiritual ancestor, passing down knowledge and righteousness that later influenced Noah's character.

companions.name: Angels Uriel and Raphael
companions.description: In some apocryphal texts, specific angels like Uriel and Raphael are mentioned as guiding Enoch on his journeys through the heavens, revealing cosmological secrets and moral lessons.

name: Enoch (Idris in Islam)

heroes.note: Other biblical figure taken to heaven without dying

description:
Enoch (Hebrew: חֲנוֹךְ, Ḥănōḵ; Arabic: إدريس, ʾIdrīs) holds a unique position across Jewish, Christian, and Islamic traditions as a figure of profound righteousness and divine favor. His story, while concise in the canonical Hebrew Bible, expands significantly in apocryphal and pseudepigraphal texts, most notably the Books of Enoch, and finds resonance in Islamic narratives where he is known as Idris. His primary distinction lies in being one of the few individuals in biblical and related lore, alongside Elijah, to be taken directly to heaven without experiencing death. This extraordinary departure from the natural order has cemented Enoch's status as a symbol of piety, divine intimacy, and eschatological hope. The brief mention in Genesis 5:24, 'Enoch walked with God; then he was no more, because God took him,' serves as a foundational point for the rich tapestry of legends and theological interpretations that have accumulated around his figure over centuries.

The Genesis account places Enoch as the seventh generation from Adam, linking him to the earliest history of humanity. His father was Jared, and he fathered Methuselah, who is famously known as the longest-lived human in the Bible. The phrase 'walked with God' (הִתְהַלֵּךְ אֱלֹהִים, *hithallek Elohim*) is crucial to understanding Enoch's character. It suggests a close, constant, and obedient relationship with the divine. This intimate communion is what ultimately leads to his 'translation,' a term used to describe his being taken to heaven without dying. The Hebrew word *laqach* (לָקַח), translated as 'took,' implies a direct and active intervention by God in Enoch's fate. The brevity of the biblical account invites speculation and further elaboration, which the later Enochic literature readily provides.

The Books of Enoch, a collection of texts not included in the Hebrew Bible or most Protestant Christian Bibles but considered canonical in Ethiopian Orthodox Christianity, offer a detailed and imaginative expansion of Enoch's story. These texts, particularly 1 Enoch (also known as Ethiopic Enoch), present Enoch as a seer and a scribe who receives divine revelations about the cosmos, the future judgment, and the activities of angels, both good and fallen. He journeys through the heavens and the earth, witnessing the secrets of creation and the consequences of sin. A central theme in 1 Enoch is the story of the Watchers (Grigori), a group of angels who descended to earth and cohabitated with human women, producing the Nephilim, giants whose wickedness corrupted the earth. Enoch acts as an intercessor on behalf of humanity, pleading with God to have mercy on the world. He also delivers God's judgment on the Watchers, foretelling their punishment in the fiery abyss. The Book of Enoch provides a complex cosmology, detailing the structure of the heavens, the movement of the stars, and the nature of angels and demons. It introduces concepts of a final judgment and the resurrection of the dead, ideas that would later become central to Jewish and Christian eschatology. The influence of 1 Enoch on the New Testament is evident in the Epistle of Jude, which directly quotes a prophecy attributed to Enoch (Jude 1:14-15, quoting 1 Enoch 1:9).

2 Enoch, also known as Slavonic Enoch, presents a different perspective on Enoch's ascent to heaven. In this text, Enoch is taken through multiple levels of heaven, where he receives instruction from angels and observes the divine realm. He is eventually transformed into an angel himself, becoming Metatron, the 'lesser YHWH' and the prince of the divine presence. This transformation highlights the theme of human potential for deification and the possibility of transcending mortal limitations. 3 Enoch, or the Hebrew Book of Enoch, further elaborates on the Metatron narrative, describing Enoch's enthronement in heaven and his role as a mediator between God and humanity. These Enochic texts demonstrate a rich and evolving tradition of speculation about the afterlife, the nature of angels, and the destiny of humanity. They reflect a period of intense religious and intellectual ferment in the Second Temple period, shaping the development of Jewish mysticism and apocalyptic thought.

In Islamic tradition, Enoch is known as Idris (إدريس), a prophet mentioned twice in the Quran. Surah Maryam (19:56-57) states, 'And mention Idris in the Book. Indeed, he was a man of truth, a prophet. And We raised him to a high station.' The Quranic account is brief, but it affirms Idris's prophetic status and his elevation to a position of honor in God's presence. Islamic commentators have elaborated on the figure of Idris, drawing parallels with the biblical Enoch and incorporating elements from other traditions. Al-Tabari, a renowned Islamic historian, describes Idris as a wise and righteous man who lived before the flood. He is credited with introducing various crafts and sciences to humanity, including writing, astronomy, and tailoring. Some Islamic traditions associate Idris with Hermes Trismegistus, the legendary figure of Hellenistic Egypt who was revered as a sage and a philosopher. This connection suggests a confluence of Jewish, Christian, and pagan influences in the development of the Idris narrative. The most significant aspect of Idris's story in Islam is his ascension to heaven. According to some accounts, during the Prophet Muhammad's Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj), Muhammad encountered Idris in the fourth heaven. This encounter underscores Idris's high spiritual rank and his proximity to God. The Islamic tradition emphasizes Idris's piety, wisdom, and his role as a guide for humanity, aligning with the broader themes of prophetic succession and divine guidance in Islam.

The symbolism of Enoch/Idris is multifaceted. His 'walking with God' represents a life of righteousness, obedience, and intimate communion with the divine. His translation to heaven symbolizes the possibility of transcending mortality and achieving eternal life. In the Books of Enoch, his role as a seer and a scribe underscores the importance of knowledge, wisdom, and the transmission of divine revelation. He acts as a bridge between the human and the divine realms, mediating between God and humanity. The themes of judgment and redemption are central to the Enochic literature, reflecting the anxieties and hopes of a community facing challenges and uncertainties. In Islamic tradition, Idris symbolizes prophetic wisdom, moral uprightness, and the pursuit of knowledge. His association with various crafts and sciences highlights the importance of human ingenuity and the development of civilization. His ascension to heaven reinforces the Islamic belief in the afterlife and the rewards awaiting the righteous. The cultural significance of Enoch/Idris lies in his enduring appeal across different religious traditions. He represents a universal longing for immortality, a desire for divine intimacy, and a hope for a just and righteous world. His story continues to inspire and challenge believers to live lives of piety, wisdom, and service to humanity.

Enoch's legacy extends beyond religious texts. He has been a subject of artistic representation, literary exploration, and theological debate for centuries. His story has been interpreted in various ways, reflecting the changing cultural and intellectual landscapes of different eras. He remains a figure of fascination and inspiration, embodying the human quest for meaning, purpose, and connection with the divine. The enduring appeal of Enoch/Idris underscores the power of myth and legend to shape our understanding of the world and our place within it.

**Domains:** righteousness, prophecy, secrets, heavens, judgment

**Epithets:**
*   The Seventh from Adam
*   The Righteous One
*   The Translated (Hebrew: *Ha-Mutaq* traditionally attributed)
*   The Prophet (Arabic: *an-Nabī*)
*   The Seer of Visions

worshipPractices.name: Ethical Living and Righteous Conduct
worshipPractices.description: While Enoch himself is not directly worshipped, his life serves as a model for righteous living. Followers strive to emulate his piety, seeking to live in accordance with divine will and maintain a close relationship with God through ethical behavior.

worshipPractices.name: Study of Sacred Texts
worshipPractices.description: In Jewish tradition, particularly within mystical circles, studying texts associated with Enoch, such as the Book of Enoch, is considered a way to gain insight into divine mysteries. These texts offer glimpses into the heavenly realms and are believed to provide guidance for spiritual advancement.

festivals.name: No specific festival
festivals.description: There are no specific festivals dedicated solely to Enoch in Jewish tradition. His memory is honored through the broader observance of religious holidays and the emphasis on ethical conduct throughout the year.

cultural.modernLegacy: Enoch's influence persists in modern literature, art, and film, often as a symbol of divine connection and righteous living. His story inspires themes of spiritual journeys, the pursuit of knowledge, and the potential for human transformation through faith. Furthermore, Enoch’s role as a prophet and intermediary between God and humanity resonates in contemporary discussions about spirituality and ethical leadership.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Enoch and Ganymede were mortals taken up to the heavens due to their exceptional qualities. Ganymede became the cupbearer of the gods, while Enoch was taken by God and did not experience death.

While Odin is a deity and Enoch is a mortal, both figures are associated with gaining secret knowledge through otherworldly journeys. Enoch ascended to heaven and gained divine wisdom, while Odin sought knowledge from various sources, including the Well of Urd.

Both Thoth and Enoch are associated with writing, wisdom, and divine secrets. Thoth was the scribe of the gods, recording knowledge, while Enoch is credited with writing books of prophecy and wisdom received during his heavenly ascent.

associations.name: Star
associations.significance: Enoch's ascent to heaven and transformation into an angel connects him to the celestial realm and the stars' eternal light.
Enoch lived 365 years, a solar number often associated with completeness and a full cycle, symbolizing his complete life of righteousness.

associations.name: Translation
associations.significance: Enoch's 'translation' signifies his unique and direct ascension to heaven without experiencing death, highlighting his exceptional spiritual status.

associations.name: Divine Scribe
associations.significance: As the scribe of God, Enoch embodies wisdom, knowledge, and the accurate recording of divine decrees and secrets.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White represents purity, righteousness, and the angelic state Enoch achieved upon his transformation.

associations.name: Air
associations.significance: Air symbolizes Enoch's spiritual journey and ascent to higher realms, representing his connection to the divine and ethereal.

associations.name: Phoenix
associations.significance: Like the phoenix, Enoch undergoes a transformative 'death' and rebirth, rising to a higher plane of existence, symbolizing immortality.

extendedContent.title: Early Life & Divine Ascent: A Tailor's Path to Prophecy
extendedContent.content: While the Hebrew Bible offers only a brief account of Enoch, stating that he "walked with God; and he was not, for God took him" (Genesis 5:24), later traditions, particularly in the Books of Enoch, flesh out his character. He is described not as a warrior or king, but as a man deeply devoted to righteousness and wisdom. Some Midrashic sources depict Enoch as a simple tailor, a profession symbolizing precision and meticulousness, reflecting his careful observance of divine law. This resonates with the Islamic portrayal of Idris, where he is also associated with crafts and knowledge, particularly writing and astronomy. The significance lies in the idea that spiritual elevation is not limited to the elite or powerful; even a common man, through piety and dedication, can achieve divine favor. This early life, though shrouded in mystery, sets the stage for Enoch's extraordinary journey and eventual translation to heaven, a theme echoed in both Jewish and Islamic accounts. The humble origins emphasize the transformative power of faith and good deeds, making Enoch/Idris a relatable figure for believers across different cultures.

extendedContent.title: The Book of Enoch: Unveiling Celestial Secrets and Doomsday Visions
extendedContent.content: The pseudepigraphal Book of Enoch (1 Enoch), a collection of texts attributed to Enoch, profoundly shapes his mythological persona. This book reveals Enoch's celestial journeys, guided by angels, where he witnesses the wonders of creation, including the secrets of astronomy, meteorology, and the angelic hierarchy. He is shown the future, including the coming judgment and the fate of the righteous and the wicked. A crucial aspect of the Book of Enoch is its depiction of the Watchers, fallen angels who corrupted humanity by teaching forbidden knowledge and interbreeding with human women, resulting in the Nephilim. Enoch serves as an intercessor, pleading with God on behalf of these fallen angels, though his efforts are ultimately unsuccessful. The Book of Enoch provides a comprehensive cosmology and eschatology, shaping Jewish and early Christian thought regarding the afterlife, demonology, and the end times. Its enduring popularity reflects a deep human fascination with the hidden realms and the mysteries of divine judgment.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence: A Bridge Between Worlds and Eras
extendedContent.content: Enoch's legacy extends far beyond his brief mention in Genesis. The Book of Enoch, despite its exclusion from the Hebrew Bible canon, exerted considerable influence on Jewish apocalyptic literature, including the Book of Daniel and the Dead Sea Scrolls. Early Christians, including the authors of the New Testament epistles of Jude and 2 Peter, cite the Book of Enoch as authoritative. In Islam, Idris holds a high rank among the prophets, known for his wisdom, knowledge, and righteousness. He is credited with introducing various crafts and sciences to humanity. The shared reverence for Enoch/Idris across these Abrahamic religions highlights his enduring appeal as a figure who transcends earthly limitations and embodies divine favor. His story serves as a reminder of the importance of piety, knowledge, and righteousness in achieving spiritual elevation. The enduring narratives surrounding Enoch/Idris continue to inspire and shape religious beliefs and practices across different cultures and eras.

extendedContent.title: Death & Afterlife: Translation, not Demise
extendedContent.content: The most striking aspect of Enoch's story is his departure from the earthly realm. Genesis 5:24 states that "Enoch walked with God; and he was not, for God took him." This cryptic verse has been interpreted as a divine translation, meaning Enoch did not experience death in the traditional sense but was taken directly into God's presence. This notion is further elaborated in later traditions, which describe Enoch's ascent to heaven, where he becomes Metatron, the angel of the divine presence. This transformation signifies Enoch's complete integration with the divine realm and his elevation to a position of immense power and authority. The concept of translation, as opposed to death, offers a unique perspective on the afterlife, suggesting that righteous individuals can bypass the limitations of mortality and achieve direct communion with God. The Islamic narrative similarly emphasizes Idris's ascension to heaven, solidifying his status as a divinely favored figure who transcends the boundaries of earthly existence.

achievements: Walked with God and did not die, but was taken by God (Genesis 5:24).
Received divine revelations and secrets of the cosmos.
Traveled through the heavens and the underworld in visionary journeys.
Transcribed celestial knowledge into books, including astronomical and angelogical information.
Delivered messages of judgment and hope to humanity and fallen angels.
Ascended to heaven and was transformed into the angel Metatron in some traditions.
Taught humanity writing, astronomy, and other sciences (Islamic tradition).
shortDescription: A righteous figure in Jewish, Christian, and Islamic traditions who ascended to heaven and received divine knowledge. Known as Idris in Islam.
subtitle: The Righteous Scribe: Ascended Prophet of Divine Knowledge
